paragraphs.text: ehpm European Federation of Associations of Health Product Manufacturers
21 October 2025

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The European Federation of Associations of Health Product Manufacturers (EHPM), registered on the EU Transparency Register (No. 65512466920-96), welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: non-compliant products originating from third countries constitute a form of unfair competition for EU-based Food Business Operators (FBOs).

paragraphs.text: EHPM fully supports the objectives of the Digital Fairness Act, which seeks to strengthen consumer protection in the digital environment by addressing manipulative online practices, misleading marketing, and non-transparent commercial communications. In this context, EHPM emphasises the importance of enhancing enforcement against misleading digital marketing and recognises that non-compliant products originating from third countries constitute a form of unfair competition for EU-based Food Business Operators (FBOs).

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: advertising via social media, including influencer marketing, is an increasingly common practice among businesses operating in the EU.

paragraphs.text: It is important to note that advertising via social media, including influencer marketing, is an increasingly common practice among businesses operating in the EU. EHPM and its members are therefore are actively engaged in developing and updating Specialised Advertising Guidelines for commercial communications on social media as well as training programmes for influencers with a particular focus on protecting minors, in line with the objectives of the Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: However, EHPM is concerned about proposals that conflate food supplements with harmful products and services, which is undermining the integrity of EU law, EFSA Opinions and could unduly burden already highly regulated compliant EU food supplements business operators, most of which are SMEs.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Food supplements are regulated and beneficial health products and should not be grouped with harmful or addictive products. Such classification is neither evidence-based nor consistent with the EU regulatory framework, which comprehensively regulates food supplements in respect of safety, composition, labelling and advertising.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Food Supplements are regulated and beneficial health products that must be distinguished from High-Risk Product Categories

paragraphs.text: Food supplements are food products that provide concentrated sources of nutrients or other ingredients with nutritional or physiological effect and are used by millions of European consumers to support their nutritional needs and health. Food supplements have science-based benefits, and their communication is regulated by EU Regulation 1924/2006 on Nutrition and Health Claims made on food, as well as authorised claims lists such as the one established by regulation 432/2012 and its amendments. For example, Iron contributes to the reduction of tiredness and fatigue, magnesium contributes to normal muscle function, vitamin C contributes to the normal function of the immune system, etc.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Food Supplements are regulated and beneficial health products that must be distinguished from High-Risk Product Categories

paragraphs.text: Moreover, food supplements are subject to rigorous safety and labelling requirements.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Food Supplements are regulated and beneficial health products that must be distinguished from High-Risk Product Categories

paragraphs.text: The EU legal framework governing food supplements is well established and robust, ensuring that European producers meet the highest safety standards.

section_path: EHPM Contribution to the European Commission Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Food Supplements are regulated and beneficial health products that must be distinguished from High-Risk Product Categories

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading or unauthorised claims made by influencers

paragraphs.text: EHPM strongly supports stricter and more consistent enforcement against misleading or unauthorised claims made by influencers. However, it is essential that EU companies which already comply with national and EU food law are not unduly burdened by new disproportionate measures.

section_path: Support Proportionate Enforcement of Influencer Marketing Rules

paragraphs.text: The priority should be to address the real challenge: the growing spread of misinformation and the easy online access to non-compliant products from third-country operators and rogue traders, which not only undermines consumer safety but also erodes public trust in compliant EU operators and creates a clear case of unfair competition.

section_path: Support Proportionate Enforcement of Influencer Marketing Rules

paragraphs.text: In this regard, EHPM calls on the European Commission to strengthen enforcement of the existing EU framework—including the Nutrition and Health Claims Regulation and the Food Information to Consumers Regulation—before introducing any new restrictions. Food supplements are already regulated foods with authorised claims, strict labelling rules, and well-established safety requirements. Treating them as harmful products, as suggested in some recent discussions, would be misleading and counterproductive.

section_path: Support Proportionate Enforcement of Influencer Marketing Rules

paragraphs.text: Enforcement should be risk-based and proportionate, targeting problematic claims and practices across all product categories, rather than categorically stigmatising regulated food supplements. This approach will more effectively protect consumers, uphold trust, and ensure a level playing field for compliant EU businesses, while closing the enforcement gap vis-à-vis third-country operators and rogue traders.

section_path: Support Proportionate Enforcement of Influencer Marketing Rules

paragraphs.text: Food supplements support public health and help reduce healthcare costs, serving to ameliorate the increasing pressures on national public health systems across the EU. 1,2

section_path: Recognise the Public Health Benefits and Healthcare Cost Reduction
Potential of Food Supplements

paragraphs.text: For instance, Calcium and vitamin D3 can be used to reduce the risk of osteoporosis, folic acid to reduce the risk of certain births defects such as spina bifida, DHA and EPA to reduce high cholesterol, abnormal blood pressure and triglycerides.

section_path: Recognise the Public Health Benefits and Healthcare Cost Reduction
Potential of Food Supplements

paragraphs.text: As such, food supplements, together with a healthy lifestyle, contribute to the improvement of citizens' health and wellbeing, reducing the financial burden of healthcare systems on Member States. Lowering diet-related risk factors for several diseases is a sustainable preventive approach, that serves the needs of the EU population first and foremost. This is particularly important in the EU, as an increasing 20 percent of the population is aged 65 and older.

section_path: Recognise the Public Health Benefits and Healthcare Cost Reduction
Potential of Food Supplements

paragraphs.text: In this regard, EHPM, call upon the EC to acknowledge the economic and societal value of food supplements in EU public health policy and discourse.

section_path: Recognise the Public Health Benefits and Healthcare Cost Reduction
Potential of Food Supplements

section_path: Recognise the Public Health Benefits and Healthcare Cost Reduction
Potential of Food Supplements

paragraphs.text: EHPM fully supports the overarching objective of strengthening enforcement against misleading digital marketing and recognise that non-compliant products originating from third countries constitute a form of unfair competition for Food Business Operators based in the EU. However, it is critical that these efforts are pursued in a way that is evidence-based, proportionate, and consistent and coherent with EU's established legal framework.

section_path: Concluding Remarks

paragraphs.text: Food supplements are highly regulated food products with longstanding and robust EU rules ensuring safety, quality, and compliance. Conflating food supplements with addictive or high-risk product categories is neither scientifically justified nor legally appropriate. Such classification risks eroding consumer trust discrediting the public health value of food supplements and undermining the competitiveness of compliant EU operators relative to third country producers.

section_path: Concluding Remarks

paragraphs.text: EHPM therefore call upon the EC to adopt a balanced, science-based approach that protects minors, enforces EU law, and safeguards both consumers and compliant EU businesses.

section_path: Concluding Remarks

paragraphs.text: The European Federation of Associations of Health Product Manufacturers (EHPM) was created in 1975 and, since then, EHPM has been the voice of the food supplement sector in Europe. With its 14 National Associations and 16 Member Companies, EHPM represents approximately 1.600 health product manufacturers and distributors, the majority of whom are small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), in 17 European countries. EHPM cooperates closely with EU institutions to support policies fostering consumer protection, innovation, and fair competition.
